preFix.title: Starch surface sizing for handmade paper
preFix.subtitle: Applying cooked starch to reduce absorbency and prepare paper for ink writing and printing
preFix.excerpt: Cooked starch applied to a dry handmade sheet fills the surface pores and reduces absorbency. Starch sizing is easier to prepare than gelatine sizing and produces a writing surface suitable for water-based inks and light wash.
preFix.sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapter on surface sizing with starch and other agents. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/oldpapermak00hunt. Hunter documents starch sizing as the traditional method in Asian papermaking traditions, particularly with rice and wheat starches.

content.text: absorbs water-based ink quickly. The ink bleeds into the fibre network rather than sitting on the surface, producing fuzzy edges on calligraphy and bleeding in watercolour wash. Starch sizing fills the surface layer with gelatinised starch molecules, slowing the ink's penetration and keeping it at the surface. The result is a harder, slightly stiffer paper that takes a calligraphy nib cleanly.
You also need: rice starch or wheat starch (kitchen-grade is suitable); a small saucepan; a flat dish or tray larger than the sheet; blotting paper; and pressing boards.
Mix one part starch with ten parts cold water in a small bowl. Stir until no lumps remain.

Heat in a saucepan over low heat, stirring continuously. The mixture will thicken suddenly at roughly 70 to 75 degrees Celsius. Continue stirring for one minute after thickening, then remove from heat.

Add three more parts cold water and stir to thin the hot mixture. The working consistency should be that of thin cream: it should run smoothly from a spoon rather than falling in lumps. Too thick a solution produces an uneven surface; too thin leaves little size behind.
Allow to cool to room temperature before sizing the sheets.
Float the sheet face-down on the surface of the size in the tray. Hold two diagonal corners and lower the sheet onto the liquid surface rather than dropping it: dropping traps air bubbles.
Allow the sheet to float for 15 to 30 seconds, then lift it by one corner and let it drain.
Lay the wet sheet between two sheets of dry blotting paper. Place in the book press at light pressure for 10 minutes.
Remove from the press and air-dry on a line or flat surface. The sheet will
slightly as it dries. When dry, calender under weight or in the press to flatten.

Let a drop of water fall onto the sized sheet from a height of 30 cm. A well-sized sheet will hold the drop on the surface for several seconds before absorbing it. An unsized sheet absorbs the drop instantly. For ink calligraphy, test a dried sized sheet with a dip pen before using it for a finished piece: if the ink bleeds or the nib catches on surface texture, size again or follow with light calendering.
